original_abstract:
Digital Twin (DT) technology has emerged as a transformative cornerstone of Industry 4.0, yet its adoption within the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) industry remains fragmented and primarily confined to the operations and maintenance phase. This paper provides a comprehensive reorientation of DT strategies by examining their application across the three primary stages of the civil engineering life cycle: planning/design, construction, and operation/maintenance. We introduce a standardized five-level scaling system to categorize DT maturity and evaluate the transition from static Building Information Modeling (BIM) to dynamic, bidirectional virtual twins. 

Through a systematic review of phase-specific methodologies, we identify critical enabling technologies—including computer vision for advanced sensing, the Internet of Things (IoT) for real-time integration, and probabilistic graphical models for uncertainty quantification. By addressing the unique challenges of each phase, such as the transient nature of construction sites and the diagnostic requirements of structural health monitoring, this work advocates for a more holistic, lifecycle-integrated approach. Ultimately, we present DT not merely as a standalone tool for monitoring, but as a synergistic framework capable of optimizing project delivery, safety, and long-term asset resilience.
humanized_abstract:
Digital Twin (DT) technology has become one of the key elements of Industry 4.0, but its application in the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) industry is still limited and mainly focused on operation and maintenance phase. This paper takes the civil engineering life cycle as the background, and based on literature review and real engineering practice, takes the whole life cycle into consideration. The whole life cycle includes the planning/design stage, the construction stage and the operation/maintenance stage. A five-level standardization maturity scale is introduced to evaluate the whole life cycle DT application. On this basis, the paper discusses the evolution path of DT from static Building Information Modeling (BIM) to dynamic and bidirectional virtual twin.

Taking a retrospective look on the existing phase-specific approaches and methods, we determine the key enabling technologies such as computer vision for sensoring, Internet of Things (IoT) for connectivity, and probabilistic graphical models for uncertainty analysis. With the understanding of the challenges in each phase of DT, from the short term character of temporary construction sites to the diagnostic nature of structural health monitoring, the paper advocates for a more integrated approach through the entire life cycle of a construction project. Finally, we argue that DT should not be seen merely as an individual tool for monitoring, but as an integrated approach that has the potential to facilitate and benefit the overall construction project management, ensuring both on-site safety during the construction phase, and on-going serviceability of the final structure.
